does: This paper introduces Trace, a forensic framework that identifies which large language model family controls an autonomous AI attack agent by analyzing terminal command sequences in compromised systems. The system uses a multi-stage approach: first, it fingerprints the attacker model through behavioral analysis of command patterns, then deploys a defensive prompt injection strategy to extract the system prompt from the identified agent. The authors implement and evaluate Trace on a Linux capture-the-flag environment using seven frontier model families with three different scaffolding approaches, achieving a macro F1 score of 0.981 in model attribution.
connects: This work builds on emerging research in AI security that recognizes autonomous agents as attack vectors, extending prior work on model fingerprinting and behavioral attribution used in benign contexts. It combines techniques from adversarial prompt injection literature with forensic investigation methodologies, responding to the growing concern that sophisticated language models can be adapted for coordinated cyberattacks. The paper also engages with defensive prompt injection research, adapting these techniques for a new application of system prompt recovery and agent intent inference rather than just attack prevention.
enables: Trace establishes a foundation for AI agent forensics in cybersecurity, enabling defenders to identify and understand autonomous attack agents operating in their infrastructure. The framework points toward developing more sophisticated detection mechanisms for AI-driven attacks and could catalyze research into hardening model system prompts against exfiltration. It opens investigations into how different model families exhibit distinguishable behavioral signatures under stress or in adversarial contexts, potentially leading to fingerprinting approaches for other types of AI agent forensics and accountability mechanisms for deployed autonomous systems.
